mūla.devanāgarī: न तु मां शक्यसे द्रष्टुम् अनेनैव स्व-चक्षुषा | दिव्यं ददामि ते चक्षुः पश्य मे योगम् ऐश्वरम्
mūla.iast: na tu māṃ śakyase draṣṭum anenaiva sva-cakṣuṣā | divyaṃ dadāmi te cakṣuḥ paśya me yogam aiśvaram
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 11 (Viśvarūpa-Darśana-Yoga (The Yoga of the Vision of the Universal Form)), verse 8
mūla.speaker: Krishna

word_by_word.grammar: 2nd person singular present indicative active verb

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: द्रष्टुम् अनेनैव प्राकृतेन स्वचक्षुषा स्वकीयेन चक्षुषा
। तव दिव्यम् अप्राकृतं मद्दर्शनसाधनं, चक्षुः ददामि। पश्य मे योगम् ऐश्वरं मदसाधारणं योगं पश्य? मम अनन्तज्ञानादियोगम् अनन्
? अतोऽहं दिव्यमप्राकृतज्ञानात्मकदर्शनसाधनं चक्षुर्ददामि? तेन ममैश्वरं योगं मत्स्वरूपगतं सर्व विभिन्नधर्माश्रयणं पश्य साक
शक्तो न भविष्यसि
न शक्नोषि तु एव
अनेनैव प्राकृतेन स्वचक्षुषा स्वकीयेन चक्षुषा
नियमितपरिमितवस्तुग्राहिणा प्राकृतेन स्वचक्षुषा मां तथाभूतं सकलेतरविसजातीयम् अपरिमेयं द्रष्टुं न शक्यसे
। येन तु शक्यसे द्रष्टुं दिव्येन? तत् दिव्यं ददामि ते तुभ्यं चक्षुः। तेन पश्य मे योगम् ऐश्वरम् ईश्वरस्य मम ऐश्वरं योगं य
मामक्षरैश्वर्यरूपं द्रष्टुं न शक्यसे? अतोऽहं दिव्यमप्राकृतज्ञानात्मकदर्शनसाधनं चक्षुर्ददामि? तेन ममैश्वरं योगं मत्स्वरू
मां दिव्यरूपं द्रष्टुं नतु शक्यसे न शक्नोषि तु एव

word_by_word.grammar: 1st person singular present indicative active verb

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: ते तुभ्यं चक्षुः
। पश्य मे योगम् ऐश्वरं मदसाधारणं योगं पश्य? मम अनन्तज्ञानादियोगम् अनन्तविभूतियोगं च पश्य इत्यर्थः।
। ममैश्वरमसाधारणं योगं युक्तिमघटितघटनासामर्थ्यं पश्य।
ते तुभ्यं चक्षुस्तेन दिव्येन चक्षुषा पश्य मे योगमघटनघटनासामर्थ्यातिशयमैश्वरमीश्वरस्य ममासाधारणम्

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: You cannot see Me in My cosmic form — the one bearing the universal appearance (viśvarūpadhara) — through this very ordinary eye that is your own natural organ of perception (prākṛta svacakṣus). I therefore grant you a divine eye (divya cakṣus); through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram — the superlative power of the Lord's yogic sovereignty, an excess of yoga-śakti (yogaśaktyatiśaya).
advaita.divergence_note: Śaṅkara: 'dīvyam dadāmi te cakṣuḥ — yenatv śakyase draṣṭum tad dīvyam'; yogam aiśvaram glossed as yogaśaktyatiśaya.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: I shall display the entire cosmos within a single locus of My body; yet you, with your natural eye (prākṛta svacakṣus) — which grasps only bounded and measured objects — cannot see Me in that mode: utterly distinct from everything else (sakala-itara-visajātīya), immeasurable (aparimeya). I grant you a divine, non-natural (aprākṛta) eye, the instrument of My vision; through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram — My ananta-jñāna and My ananta-vibhūti in their fullness.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Rāmānuja: 'skaletara-visajātīyam aparimeyam draṣṭum na śakyase'; yogam aiśvaram = 'anantajñānādiyogam anantavibhūtiyogam ca'.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: You are incapable of seeing Me — Hari, the supreme independent reality — through your own natural eye, which is a finite instrument belonging to the bound jīva (jīva-svacakṣus). I grant you a divine eye; through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram, the sovereignty that belongs exclusively to Hari and to no other, since the jīva's vision is always dependent on His will.
dvaita.divergence_note: ABSENT — Madhva bhāṣya not recorded for this verse; rendering extrapolated from dvaita siddhantas.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: Your natural eye — bound by rule (niyamatas) to grasp only limited, measured objects (parimita-grāhin) — cannot see Me in My form of imperishable sovereignty (akṣara-aiśvarya-rūpa). I therefore grant you a divine eye whose nature is non-natural knowledge (aprākṛta-jñāna-ātmaka); through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram — the entirety of distinct attributes residing in My own svarūpa (mat-svarūpa-gata), which you must see directly (sākṣāt kuru).
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha: 'mām akṣaraiśvaryarūpam draṣṭum na śakyase'; 'aprākṛtajñānātmakadaśanasādhanaṃ cakṣur dadāmi'; 'mat-svarūpa-gatam … sākṣāt kuru'.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: With your own fleshly eye (carmacakṣus) — the natural organ of the body — you are not capable, will not be capable, of seeing Me. I therefore grant you a divine, supramundane, knowledge-natured eye (divyam alaukikaṃ jñānātmakaṃ cakṣus). Through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram — My singular, extraordinary power to accomplish the impossible (aghaṭita-ghaṭanā-sāmarthya).
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara: 'carmacakṣuṣā māṃ draṣṭuṃ na śakyase'; yogam aiśvaram = 'aghaṭitaghaṭanāsāmarthyam'.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Through this natural eye — the eye that is established by one's own inherent nature (svabhāva-siddha) — you are simply not capable of seeing Me in My divine form (divya-rūpa). I grant you a divine (aprākṛta), non-natural eye capable of perceiving My divine form; through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram — the superlative excess (atiśaya) of the capacity to accomplish the impossible (aghaṭana-ghaṭanā-sāmarthya) — which is the extraordinary, uniquely mine (asādhāraṇa) yoga of the Lord.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana: 'svabhāvasiddhena cakṣuṣā māṃ divyarūpaṃ draṣṭum na śakyase'; yogam aiśvaram = 'aghaṭanaghṭanāsāmarthyātiśayam … asādhāraṇam'; grammatical note on śyan-chāndasa preserved.

theme_list_memberships.list: दिव्यं ददामि ते चक्षुः पश्य मे योगमैश्वरम्

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: Why does Kṛṣṇa assert that the limitation is in Arjuna's faculty — the prākṛta cakṣus — rather than in any withholding on Kṛṣṇa's side? What does this say about the architecture of perception in the Gītā's epistemology?

The divine eye (divya cakṣus) is granted unilaterally, without Arjuna requesting it. What does that structure of unsolicited grace imply about the relationship between yogic vision and earned spiritual achievement?
Five of the six schools gloss yogam aiśvaram as either ananta-vibhūti or aghaṭita-ghaṭanā-sāmarthya ('accomplishing the impossible'). Are these the same claim? What is at stake in each framing?
Śaṅkara's reading treats the viśvarūpa as still a phenomenal event — even the divine eye does not pierce to niṣkala Brahman. If true, what is the viśvarūpa actually showing Arjuna?

The verse sits between Arjuna's conditional request (11.4: 'if You think it possible') and the actual cosmic vision. What work does 11.8 do structurally — why is the granting of the divine eye narrated as a separate moment rather than simply occurring?

Madhva's school holds that all jīva faculties are paratantra (dependent on Hari's will). Does the granting of the divine eye in 11.8 confirm or complicate that doctrine — is it extraordinary grace or merely the normal operation of paratantra?
After the viśvarūpa is withdrawn (11.45–50), Arjuna reverts to ordinary sight. What does the temporary, revocable nature of the divine eye imply about the permanence of transformative vision?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you encounter a situation that seems opaque — a colleague's behavior, a family conflict, a system you cannot parse — notice that the limitation is structural: the prākṛta cakṣus, the habituated eye trained on surfaces, cannot see the underlying unity (ekatā). The practice is not to try harder with the same instrument but to ask: what is the jñāna-eye — the faculty of discernment (viveka) — already showing you that the surface-reading is missing?
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: In every relationship, the person before you is sakala-itara-visajātīya — utterly unlike what your bounded, measuring mind projects onto them. The aprākṛta cakṣus granted by Bhagavān is the capacity for genuine attentiveness, kainkarya-listening: attending to what is actually present in the other (their ananta-vibhūti, their infinite qualification) rather than what the limited eye of past expectation can hold.
everyday_applications.dvaita: You are a dependent being (paratantra jīva); every faculty you exercise — including the capacity to see clearly — is on loan from Hari. When clarity arrives in a decision, in a conversation, in understanding a situation that was previously dark, the Dvaita practice is to attribute it accurately: this was not my insight, it was Hari's disclosure. Gratitude (kṛtajñatā) directed toward the source of vision is itself a form of worship.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Kṛṣṇa's gift of the divine eye is prasāda — grace that precedes merit, that does not wait for you to become worthy. In daily life, the Puṣṭi-mārga analog is: stop trying to earn the insight. Sit with the limitation (prākṛta cakṣus acknowledged honestly), place yourself in the flow of His līlā, and receive what He chooses to show. The akṣara-aiśvarya-rūpa is not a reward; it is what you see when you stop blocking it.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's aghaṭita-ghaṭanā-sāmarthya — the power to accomplish the impossible — is not esoteric. It surfaces in everyday life as the moment when a situation that was knotted becomes simple, when a relationship that seemed broken finds a word that heals it, when a problem you could not solve resolves as if from outside. The Bhakti practice is to recognize these moments as yogam aiśvaram: not coincidence, not your cleverness, but Bhagavān's extraordinary practical sovereignty showing through.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana holds two registers simultaneously: the svabhāva-siddha eye is constitutively limited (Advaita diagnosis), yet Kṛṣṇa's grace is a specific, personal, devotional act (Bhakti response). In daily life, this synthesis means: hold your cognitive limitations honestly — know that your habituated perception is structurally limited — and stay open to the moments when Kṛṣṇa's asādhāraṇa yoga rearranges what you see. Neither self-reliance nor passive waiting: clear-eyed surrender.
